text: The increase in reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) during a state of oxidative stress can stimulate an increase in pro-inflammatory mediators. Reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS) cause cellular damage, which leads to the production of pro-inflammatory mediators (Slezak et al., 2015; Sylvester et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a). In addition, ROS can act as second messenger signalling molecules in activating pro-inflammatory transcription factor nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB), resulting in increased production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and adhesion factors (Ping et al., 2020; Slezak et al., 2017; Slezak et al., 2015; Sylvester et al., 2018; Venkatesulu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a). The inflammatory state induced by RONS will further increase RONS, leading to a cycle of chronic inflammation and oxidative stress (Venkatesulu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019a).
